But the reason that it's possible is because when I write to these folks, I know them like I know me. And there is such instant rapport and believability because I mean I know what they think, I know what their life experience is, I know what their day-to-day experience is, I know what their fears are, I know what their rep dreams are, I know what they say to their wife at the kitchen table at two o'clock in the morning, I got them. And so my message is so closely matched to everything going on in their heads and everything going on with them emotionally that we get inordinately great responses every time we go to them. This particular piece mailed to about 3,000 NSA members, maybe another 500 non-NSA speakers and we did almost 30 grand in business. Yeah. And the second follow-up piece is in the mail now and if history repeats itself, we'll do that same amount again. And then the third piece will trickle. So we'll probably do $70,000 worth of business with three mailings to 3,500 people. And that's exactly what I would say. And it is in perfect sync with everything going on in his head and his heart. When you got that going for you, you really gain a big advantage in copying and when you're at the opposite end of the spectrum, it's very tough to do. Like I work on a lot of cosmetic and beauty stuff, very hard. I mean first of all, we're all guys in the room, so if anybody in here understands women, I'd love to know about it on a break. And frankly, I don't think women understand women either, so that's the solution. And so to create for them is tough. But the one thing I got to do is I got to read everything they read and I got to watch everything they watch. And I got to, so if I'm working on a cosmetic or skincare promotion, I got to do everything I can to crawl into their heads. So that month, man, I'm reading Cosmo, I'm reading New Woman, I'm reading Working Woman, I'm reading the Lifestyle sections of the newspaper, I'm watching the daytime talk shows, I'm watching Soaps, I'm watching stuff I would not necessarily ordinarily watch. I'm listening in on conversations that normally I would deliberately tune out. I mean, because I got to try and be able now to communicate with them in a way that they'll respond to. You've got to be able to talk their language, you guys just know what they're thinking. So, you know, point 12 on my list is one of the things I think smart copywriters do is is before you start to write, get a hand on what you know about your customer. I usually make a list. Some people do some five cards, whatever, but you drain your brain of everything you know about your customer. What do I know about this customer? What kind of homes do they live in? What kind of neighborhoods do they live in? What kind of magazines do they read? What kind of TV programs do they watch? You know, do they stay up late at night? Do they go to bed early? You know, I'm most often married, I'm most often single. What age are they? Do they go to college? Do they barely get through high school? Do they work in blue college jobs? Do they work in white college jobs? Do they shop at K-Mark? Do they shop at Newman Marcus? Do they go out to dinner once a week? Do they go to the theater? Do they go to the movies? Do they go to Blockbuster and rent a video or come home and get a pizza? I mean, what's going on with these people? And so now you've got a list of everything you know about this customer. The next thing is to try and crystallize that into a person so that you are writing to an individual you can visualize and talk with rather than writing to them. He does that because the hotel's your business. No problem. I don't remember where things are. But at the end, he explains what the average guy is like, and what he thinks he is, what he wants, and I guess it's before he's ever done anything like that. And then, and then he writes that. I don't see it called a chapter title, but it may very well be in here. You know, in a market where I understand that I'll zero in on somebody, on an actual person that I know who represents most of the list. I did some copy for a while for a company that sold a package of services to aerospace industry middle manager types. And it so happens when I was in high school, I dated a gal whose father was a middle manager, like a $40,000 year executive at Goodyear Aerospace in Akron, Ohio. And this guy, he was, the guy's a classic, I mean just a classic human being. This guy, they lived about a half an hour from the Goodyear Aerospace. This guy, I mean, he drove the same way, you know, the same path. You could set your watch by when the back of his head appeared in the day window of the house at the end of the day when he came home and sat down with the newspaper and his one drink to fill out himself. Because at 542, I mean not 543, not 544, his head will be in that window. That's how predictable this human being was. Friday night, every Friday night upstairs to watch Wall Street Week with Lewis Ruckheiser, you know, and play with his crappy little stock portfolio. He was right about the dinner once a month, every month, whether she needed it or not, you know, to either the sizzler or Bob's big boy. I mean this guy, you know, and when I started to do this copy, I remember this guy, you know, when I got him in my head because I mean I knew him, you know, I mean I studied this guy for like two years, I mean he drove a Ram or you know, this guy was just a classic. And I mean I wrote to this guy because I really knew this guy. And we got phenomenal response rates, you know, they were just mystified by it. And it was because of this, it was because I was able to really get a lock on who this guy was when we were trying to sell. I didn't sell a guy like that by the way. I didn't know, and I really... Well, first of all, you get a very analytical, evidence driven, you know, approach. And you need to lean into his desire for order and organization and predictability. I mean this is a guy who treasures everything staying the same. You know, and you just have to understand that. And now what you're going to sell him has to give him more of that and not disrupt him in any way, shape, or form. They're very interesting characters. And of course, totally the opposite of us with babies that you may have some of this personality in you. But... Not that particular. You can't be an entrepreneur. But you know, I remember one of the letters, you know, I had a whole tour's repair and raps in and about a story about this guy coming home from a business trip and his wife had rearranged the furniture. And how furious he was and how aggravating that was. You know, why do women insist on doing this and da da da da da da da da da da da? And I mean, and I had him, has that even worked? This guy came home and one thing was moved into the house. You know, he'd be preserved. Yeah, good thing. And so you got... When you can write to that guy, you know, a lot of the blue collar opportunity stuff, I had the white collar, but the blue collar stuff that I'll do for clients, there was a guy who worked for me for five years who every time I see Al Bundy on Very Good Children I take a disguise. This guy's life is just like, so I'll write to Al Bundy. I mean, I'll write to this guy. And if you are going to write to some blue collar markets, you definitely want to tune in and you want to watch, I'm married with a bunch of old children for a while and you want to watch Rose Rose Ann. I mean, there's a new show, Grace Under Fire, which ABC has on. I think it's after Rose Ann, if I'm not mistaken, but it's another female stand-up comic who they've built a show around. And she's a divorced mother with kids in the neighborhood and it's very blue collar and it's a little feminist militant without being humorless. And so I'll watch that stuff. I'll tape the shows and watch five or six of them before I'm going to write to a blue collar market. I'll go on a Sunday and spend a couple hours in Kmart just kind of watching people and seeing how they behave to get into that person because they're a very different person. In infomercials, our U.S. Gold Show and the show I did for the role, that is the Master in Graving Show. That's a different person that responds to your ads or responds to my Jeff Fall ad. It's a different guy. It's a real blue collar, works with his hands, fixes his own car guy. And that's not me by nature. So I don't dare write to me. And there's nobody really in my environment who's like that. So I've got to go get into that mentally to be able to write to that. This is sort of a fuzzy question, but who are you when you're writing to them? Are you you or are you? You're just out of the picture. I mean, I don't even... I mean, I try to just get out of the way entirely. So you become them writing to them? Yeah. Okay. Yeah. Okay. I mean, that's the utopia. You know, that's what happens here. So the trick is how to duplicate that in a place where it's not natural. Here it's easy. So now I'm over here. How do you duplicate this in a place that's not natural? Going and getting the testimonials is very useful before you do anything else because it gets you... I did a series of direct mail letters and a promotional video, not an infomercial with air on TV, but one that gets sent out in the mail for a company that manufactures truck trucks. And their...the tarpick systems are sold to like city fleets and companies that help grab on shit to keep the stones from flying out over the road. Well, what the hell? I don't know. You know, I know some shit, you know, about any of this. I mean, it's a great mystery to me, you know. And I certainly don't know the customer, but we take testimonials for a couple of days. Well, by the time the two days were over, I had a pretty good handle on this customer, you know, who this...I know who the supervisor was who was making the buying choice because we interviewed big 30 or 40 of those and they're all the same. I mean, after you've done 10 of them, you start this...there's a formulaic person here who winds up in this job. And the drivers who actually drive the trucks after we did 12 or 14 of those, I had a handle on that person too. Now I could go back and write copy and have it work. And you're tempted to shortcut all this, but if you do, you're not going to get spectacular results. You may be purely through formula and form able to get acceptable results, but if you want spectacular results, you've got to go through these steps. And then you always have to battle to remove yourself. You go hard. I'm just going to say that I was my partner, it was...but we're in Los Angeles, you know, we're talking about Michigan somewhere now. Yeah, it's...and you've got to think, you know, do your customers live in small towns or big cities? Big difference there. Big life experience difference. Big, you know, they just live differently. Weekends are entirely different in small town America than they are in metropolitan America. I mean, when you're writing this, you've been doing it so long, I'm sure it's like a second major, but at what point do you say, stop, am I doing...have I followed all these forms and there's just kind of so natural that you don't have to do that? Oh, no, I go back through...I mean, I have my own little pile of stuff, including the L'Occitane de la Sellerie, which really does summarize 28th...that I go back through and try and check myself against it. Because now there's a...now I do sit down at first draft. I mean, I sit down and write. Yeah. But then I go back and check and see, did we do this, did we do that? Oh, look, yeah, here's a place where we could have done this. Because you can't internalize it all. The whole preparatory stage of getting in...trying to become one of them, that's automatic now. I do that every time, unless I can't. You know, unless the client has me under such a gun, or the project is so time-sensitive that it's just impossible. But otherwise, I want to take the time to try and become one of...you know, become mentally with them. Do you have other people looking for that? Yeah, not many. I'll do a couple of things. If I have access to the market, to the prospect, I may let some prospects look at the copy. Say, read this and just tell me the first thing that comes into your mind, what do you think about all this? And really there's a couple of things that I'm looking for. One is, I'm looking for three or four of them saying the same thing. Gee, I don't get this. Or being distracted by something. Hey, I really disagree with that whole thing. And now they've gotten hung up there instead of continuing to say all about this. But the other thing that I'm looking for more in that case is whether or not they ask how they can get the product. See, because the bottom line is, not what they think about the advertising, but will they buy. This piece, we got a fair number of complaint letters from non-buyers from within NSA, objecting to a variety of things. That was pretty close to what you did with the logo. Yeah, I did get a little plaque over that. And I look at that out of curiosity, but that doesn't affect now what I do with the second letter. We also got complaints from buyers. And I was always fine, entertaining. And like I got one long six-page letter from a guy just tearing this thing apart. And you're a complete embarrassment to the profession, but he bought. He's kind of deck with the left. Yeah, so now that's real funny. It is. But the main thing I look for is that he bought. And so if I was having this read by five or six speakers ahead of time, the main thing I'm looking for is a three or four mask. When's the product that'll be available? Can I get it? Are you going to get a deal on it? Now, no, we're on the right track. If everybody says what I'm talking about, my copy, good or bad. Gee, we really love it or we really hate it, but nobody wants to buy the product. Now I know I'm in trouble. The second group of people that I'll let look at it are a handful of people who I know really understand copy. Or somebody who is more specialized in a given category than I. Like generally, if I'm doing cosmetic or skin care stuff, I'll have Loretta Duffy look at it before I finish. I don't know if you know Loretta, but Loretta did one of the most successful skin care ads ever to work in the tabloids. And she works for Frank Robinson with Fancy Quad deal. And she's very sharp in the area of skin care, cosmetic weight loss. She does not sell women. So I'll let her look at it and I'll, I mean, I'd buy everything she says, but I'll at least respectfully consider her comments. You know, I'll sometimes have Halbert look at stuff. And, you know, and there's like five or six of us that'll kind of do that back and forth for each other. But there's not a lot of people that I would do that with. Because there's not that many out there? Yeah, there's not that many out there. And everyone's forgiving you. Well, yeah, yeah, yeah. Well, you haven't understand, yeah, what other stuff they, what other baggage they come to the table with at the time that they're giving you an opinion. What biases they bring, what experience they bring, is their own ego in the way, you know, all that kind of stuff. You know, I'll look at people's copy and most people can't accept their ego's aside to do this. I'll look at somebody's copy and say, well, you know, it's certainly not the way I would have done it. But it can work the way you've done it. That's a bottom line. Not many people can do that. You know, and then the real answer is, if you're real smart, you'll take the one you did and the one I might do and you'll split test them. You know, I mean, I got a client right now that I did, we did three campaigns for them for three different seminar programs. With one, I doubled the numbers that come in the room. Mine is a clear, profound Dan's a hero winner. One of the other ones, I can't crack, I've taken three attempts at it and I get the same results he's always got. Well, some guy who's his own office wrote a piece and the damn thing did 25% better than the control or my three attempts. Now, I looked at that piece and it clearly is, I mean, you know, because of what do you think? I said, well, obviously it's not the way I would have done it because we got 40 I did, you know, and none of them match. So it's not the way I would have done it. But now that I look at it, it could work and you should test it and then go, it works. So it's hard to find somebody to critique your stuff who can get themselves out of it in order to do that. I mean, there's more than one way to be right, you know. What do you want someone to be able to do? Tell me a base I missed that is direct response proven. Or tell me where I've gotten too cute for my own good. Which we're all capable of doing. The 14th point, which kind of is in the same category, it's what's on the same page, is you give yourself a little edge when you have the timing capability to do it to link to what's already on their minds. This is the end of side one. Please turn the cassette over for side two.
